script: Good evening, Mr. Thompson. I'm Dr. Chen. I've been reviewing your chart, and it looks like you've been struggling with some persistent reflux for a long time. I see you're on high-dose Omeprazole. Can you tell me more about what's been happening lately?

Thanks, Doctor. Yeah, honestly, the Omeprazole... it just doesn't seem to touch it anymore. Every single night, regardless of what I eat, I wake up with this burning in my throat. It's like battery acid. I'm taking 40 milligrams twice a day, but I'm still miserable.
That's a very high dose to still be having breakthrough symptoms. Have you noticed any other changes? Any trouble swallowing, or the feeling of food getting stuck?

Actually, yes. Over the last month, if I have something like steak or dry chicken, it feels like it hesitates right behind my breastbone for a few seconds before going down. It's starting to worry me.
That 'hesitation' is called dysphagia, and in the context of chronic reflux, it's something we take very seriously. Have you had any weight loss, or have you been coughing at night?
I've lost about seven pounds, mostly because I'm scared to eat too much. I do cough a lot at night, usually when I've just laid down. My wife says it sounds like I'm choking.
Nocturnal regurgitation and dysphagia are significant. Do you have a family history of esophageal issues?
My father passed away from esophageal cancer when he was 62. He had bad heartburn his whole life too. That's really why I'm here. I'm terrified.

I understand. Given your long history of GERD, the lack of response to PPIs, and your family history, we need to look for Barrett’s esophagus or any structural changes. I'm going to perform an upper endoscopy, or EGD, as soon as possible.

Yes, we will take multiple biopsies from the distal esophagus to check for intestinal metaplasia, which is what defines Barrett's. I'm also going to place a BRAVO pH capsule during the procedure to measure exactly how much acid is hitting that tissue over a 48-hour period.
Is there anything I should do in the meantime?
Continue your Omeprazole, but try to avoid any meals within four hours of bedtime. Use a wedge pillow to keep your head elevated. We'll get this scheduled for Tuesday morning. Any questions?
No, Doctor. I just want some answers. Thank you.
We'll get them. Stop by the scheduler on your way out.
Hello, Sarah. It's good to see you again, though I wish it was under better circumstances. I see from your message that you're concerned about some abdominal pain returning?
Hi Dr. Chen. Yeah, I'm really frustrated. It feels exactly like it did back in 2021 before my surgery. That sharp, crampy pain in the lower right side. It's almost constant now.
I'm sorry to hear that. You had an ileocecal resection two years ago. Since the surgery, have you been taking your Humira regularly?

I haven't missed a single dose. I do the injections every two weeks like you said. But for the last month, I'm having four or five liquid stools a day, and I'm seeing some mucus... and maybe a little blood.
Blood and mucus with RLQ pain after resection is often a sign of recurrence at the anastomosis site. Any joint pain or skin rashes lately?
Actually, my knees and ankles have been really stiff in the mornings. I didn't think it was related.
Crohn's can definitely cause extra-intestinal symptoms like arthritis. Have you felt particularly tired or short of breath?
Exhausted. I can barely make it through the workday. I feel like I'm just dragging my feet.
This sounds like a clinical flare. We need to assess the extent of inflammation. I'm ordering a CT Enterography to visualize the bowel wall and check for any stricturing at the surgical join.
Does this mean I'll need another surgery?

Not necessarily. We might need to optimize your medication. I'll check your Humira trough levels and antibodies to see if your body is clearing the drug too fast. We'll also check a fecal calprotectin.

It's a stool test that measures a protein released by white blood cells in the gut. It's a very reliable marker for active Crohn's inflammation. If it's high, we'll likely need to switch you to a different biologic, perhaps Stelara or Skyrizi.
I just want to feel normal again. Is there anything I can eat that helps?

During a flare, stick to a low-fiber, 'white' diet. Think plain pasta, white rice, and lean proteins. Avoid raw vegetables and seeds for now as they can irritate the inflamed areas. We'll talk more once the CT results are in.
Hang in there, Sarah. We'll get this under control.
Good morning, David. You're looking a bit pale today. My nurse mentioned you're having a significant increase in bowel frequency?
Doctor, it's terrible. I'm going twelve, maybe fifteen times a day. It's almost all blood and urgency. I can't even leave the house because I'm afraid I won't make it to a bathroom in time.
Fifteen times a day with gross hematochezia is a medical emergency. Are you having any fevers or a racing heart?
I feel like my heart is beating out of my chest just walking from the car. I've had some low-grade fevers at night, maybe 100.2.
That's a sign of systemic inflammation. You've been on Mesalamine, and we tried a Prednisone taper last month. How did you do on the steroids?
When I was on 40 milligrams, I felt great. But as soon as I hit 20, everything came back worse than before. I'm currently at 20 and I'm drowning here.

This is what we call 'steroid-dependent' or 'steroid-refractory' colitis. Given the frequency and the systemic symptoms, I'm actually going to recommend we admit you to the hospital today for IV steroids.
The hospital? Is it that bad?
Yes. We need to stabilize you with IV hydration and high-dose Solu-Medrol. We also need to rule out secondary infections like C. diff or CMV which can complicate a flare.
What happens if the IV steroids don't work?

If we don't see a significant drop in stool frequency within 72 hours, we'll perform a 'rescue' therapy. That usually means either Infliximab or Cyclosporine. We want to avoid a colectomy, which is the surgical removal of the colon, at all costs.
Neither do we. That's why we're acting aggressively now. I'll have the nurse start an IV here for some fluids, and we'll coordinate your admission across the street. Are you okay with that?
If it stops the bleeding, yes. Thank you.
Of course. We'll get you through this.
Hello, Mrs. Gable. We're following up on your Celiac diagnosis from two years ago. How have you been feeling on the gluten-free diet?
That's the problem, Doctor. I feel like I'm doing everything right, but I'm still so bloated. I have diarrhea almost every morning, and my energy levels are zero. I'm exhausted all the time.

I'm looking at your recent blood work. Your hemoglobin is 10.2, which confirms you're still quite anemic. This suggests you might not be absorbing iron properly in the duodenum. Are you sure there isn't any hidden gluten in your diet?
I'm obsessive about it. I don't eat out, I have a separate toaster, I check every label. It's exhausting. I don't understand why I'm not getting better.
Sometimes gluten can hide in unexpected places—lipsticks, medications, or even some 'gluten-free' oats that are cross-contaminated. Have you noticed any itchy skin rashes?
A little bit on my elbows, but I thought it was just dry skin.

That could be Dermatitis Herpetiformis, which is a skin manifestation of Celiac. Given your symptoms and persistent anemia despite a reported strict diet, we need to distinguish between accidental exposure and 'refractory' Celiac disease.

It's a rare condition where the gut doesn't heal even in the absence of gluten. But before we go there, I'm going to have you meet with our specialized GI dietitian for a top-to-bottom audit of your kitchen and diet.

I'm also ordering a repeat EGD with duodenal biopsies. We need to see if the villi in your small intestine have actually started to regrow or if they're still flat. If they're still flat and your TTG-IgA levels are high, we'll know gluten is still getting in somehow.
And if the TTG levels are low but the biopsies are still bad?
Then we consider refractory Celiac, and we may need to discuss immunosuppressants. But let's take it one step at a time. Labs first, then the dietitian, then the scope.
Thank you, Doctor. I really appreciate you taking this seriously.
Good afternoon, Mike. It’s been a while. How have you been managing?

Not great, Dr. Chen. My stomach is a mess. Every time I eat, I get this dull, gnawing pain right in the center of my upper belly. It goes straight through to my back. It makes me not want to eat at all.
That pain pattern—epigastric radiating to the back—is very typical for chronic pancreatitis. How are your bowel movements? Have you noticed anything unusual about them?
This is embarrassing, but they're... they're oily. Often they float, and they're incredibly foul-smelling. I have to flush the toilet twice just to get rid of the residue. It's really gross.

Don't be embarrassed; that's actually a vital clinical sign called steatorrhea. It means your pancreas isn't producing enough enzymes to break down the fats in your food. We've had you on Creon, 24,000 units with meals. Are you taking it with every single snack and meal?
I try to, but I'm still losing weight. I've lost twelve pounds in the last two months. I feel like the Creon isn't doing anything.

You're likely under-dosed. 24,000 units is a starting dose, but for someone with your symptoms and weight loss, we need to be much more aggressive. I'm going to triple your dose to 72,000 units with every full meal, and 36,000 with any snacks.
Seventy-two thousand? That’s a lot of pills.
It is, but the goal is to stop the malabsorption. I'm also worried about your long-term nutrition. Have you had any issues with your blood sugar lately?
I've been feeling a bit more thirsty than usual, and I'm peeing more at night. Why?

Chronic pancreatitis can damage the insulin-producing cells in the pancreas too, leading to 'Type 3c' diabetes. I'm going to check your A1c today. I also want to check your levels of fat-soluble vitamins—A, D, E, and K—since you haven't been absorbing fats well.
Okay. And the pain? Is there anything else for that?

We'll try to manage it with the enzyme optimization first, as that sometimes helps the 'feedback loop' that causes pain. If not, we may need to discuss a celiac plexus block, but let's try the higher Creon dose first. Are you still able to stay away from alcohol?
Three years stone-cold sober, Doctor. I'm never going back there.
I’m very proud of you for that. Alcohol is the leading cause of flares, so staying sober is the best thing you can do for your health. I'll see you back in four weeks to check your weight.
Thank you, Dr. Chen. I'll start the new dose today.
Good morning, Mr. Miller. I'm glad your daughter was able to bring you in today. How have things been at home?
I'm okay... I think. A bit tired. I keep losing my glasses.
(Turning to daughter) Small changes like that are important. Have you noticed any significant confusion or changes in his sleep patterns?
Yes, Doctor. He's been awake half the night roaming the house, and then he sleeps all day. Yesterday, he tried to use his car keys to open the microwave. It's like he's not fully there.

That's a classic sign of 'sleep-wake reversal' and cognitive slippage, which in a patient with Child-Pugh B cirrhosis, points directly to hepatic encephalopathy. Mr. Miller, let me see your hands. Bring them up like you're stopping traffic...

Yes. I'm seeing a flickering motion, which we call asterixis, or 'liver flap.' It happens because of the buildup of ammonia in your blood that your liver isn't clearing. Have you noticed your skin or eyes looking more yellow lately?
My eyes... maybe a little bit. And my belly feels like I swallowed a basketball.

You have significant ascites, which is fluid accumulation in the abdomen. We need to manage both the ammonia and the fluid. I'm prescribing Lactulose—two tablespoons three times a day. The goal is to have three soft bowel movements per day to flush out the toxins.

It should help significantly. I'm also adding Rifaximin, which is an antibiotic that stays in the gut and kills the bacteria that produce the ammonia. For the fluid, we'll start Spironolactone, but we need to monitor your electrolytes closely.
Does this mean my liver is failing completely?

It means your liver is struggling. We need to start talking about a referral to a transplant center for a formal evaluation. In the meantime, no salt, and absolutely no alcohol. Even one drink could be catastrophic now.
We'll make sure of it. What about the fluid in his belly?

If the diuretics don't start moving it within a week, we'll have him come in for a therapeutic paracentesis to drain the fluid. Let's schedule a follow-up for next Thursday. Keep a log of his bowel movements and whether the confusion is clearing up.
Three times a day... okay. Thank you, Doctor.
Hello, Leo. I understand you had a bit of a scare at dinner last night?

It was terrifying, Dr. Chen. I was eating a piece of steak, and it just... stopped. Right at the bottom of my throat. I couldn't swallow my spit, and I felt like I was going to choke. It took about two hours of drinking water and jumping up and down before it finally passed.

That's a classic case of 'food impaction,' and it's almost always a sign of a structural or inflammatory issue in the esophagus. I see in your history that you have asthma and some seasonal allergies?
Yeah, I've had asthma since I was a kid, and I'm allergic to peanuts and tree nuts.

That profile—younger male with 'atopic' history like asthma and allergies—makes me highly suspicious of Eosinophilic Esophagitis, or EoE. Have you noticed yourself chewing your food for a long time or needing water to 'wash down' every bite?
My girlfriend always makes fun of me for being the last one finished eating. I guess I do chew forever because I'm subconsciously worried about things getting stuck.

That's a very common adaptation. EoE is an allergic inflammatory condition where white blood cells called eosinophils build up in the lining of the esophagus, causing it to narrow and become less elastic. We need to confirm this with an endoscopy.

We'll look for longitudinal furrows, rings, or white exudates. Most importantly, we'll take biopsies. If we see more than 15 eosinophils per high-power field under the microscope, the diagnosis is confirmed.
What's the treatment? Is it more allergy shots?

Actually, we have two main paths. One is 'swallowed' steroids—you take a Budesonide slurry or an inhaler but swallow the medicine instead of breathing it in. It coats the esophagus. The other is the 'Six-Food Elimination Diet'—getting rid of milk, wheat, eggs, soy, nuts, and fish.
Six foods? That's half my diet!

It’s tough, but it's the most effective way to identify the trigger without drugs. We'll start with the endoscopy first. Please stick to very soft foods and liquids until then. No more steak for now, okay?
Got it. Soft foods only. Thanksgiving is going to be rough.
Good morning, Mrs. Vance. I’ve been reading through the notes from your primary care doctor. You’ve been having significant trouble swallowing for almost a year?

Yes, Doctor. It started slowly, just with meat and bread. But now, even water seems to sit in my throat and then gurgle back up. At night, if I lay flat, undigested food from dinner comes back into my mouth. It's disgusting, and I've lost twenty pounds because I'm just miserable.

Weight loss and regurgitation of 'undigested' food, especially with difficulty swallowing both solids and *liquids*, points away from a simple narrowing and more toward a motility disorder. Have you noticed any chest pain?
I get these sharp spasms in my chest, right behind the breastbone. Sometimes I think I'm having a heart attack, but my cardiologist said my heart is perfect.

Those 'non-cardiac chest pains' are often esophageal spasms. This combination strongly suggests Achalasia. It's a condition where the lower esophageal sphincter—the valve into the stomach—fails to relax, and the esophagus itself stops pumping food down.
Is there a test for that?

The gold standard is a test called Esophageal Manometry. We'll pass a thin, pressure-sensitive tube through your nose and down into your esophagus while you take sips of water. It measures the strength and coordination of your swallows.

It’s a bit strange, but it only takes about twenty minutes and gives us the definitive answer. We'll also do a 'Timed Barium Swallow' where you drink a chalky liquid and we watch it under X-ray. In Achalasia, the liquid just sits there, looking like a 'bird's beak' at the bottom.

We can't restore the normal pumping motion, but we can fix the valve. We have three main options: we can stretch it with a balloon, inject it with Botox, or do a procedure called POEM—Peroral Endoscopic Myotomy—where we actually cut the muscle of that valve to let gravity do the work.

POEM is generally the best long-term solution for someone your age. It's done endoscopically, so no outside incisions. But first, we need that manometry data to be 100% sure. Let's get that scheduled for Monday.
Hello, Maria. I see you've had a rough couple of months. I'm looking at your A1c from last week, and it was 8.8. Your diabetes has been a bit difficult to control lately?

It's impossible, Dr. Chen. My sugar is high one minute, then it crashes. And my stomach... I'm so bloated. I eat three bites of breakfast and I feel like I've eaten a Thanksgiving turkey. I have no appetite.
That 'early satiety' is a hallmark symptom. Are you having any nausea or vomiting?
Almost every night. I'll wake up at two or three in the morning and vomit, and honestly, Doctor, it looks exactly like the dinner I ate at seven PM. It's like my stomach isn't even moving it.

Vomiting undigested food hours later is highly suggestive of Gastroparesis—literally, 'paralyzed stomach.' Given your long history of diabetes, the high blood sugar has likely damaged the vagus nerve that controls your stomach muscles.
Is there a cure? Can I just take a pill?

The most important 'cure' is tighter blood sugar control, because high sugars directly slow down gastric emptying. We can try a medication called Metoclopramide, or Reglan, to help the stomach contract. But it does have a 'black box warning' for potential neurological side effects like tremors, so we monitor it very closely.
That sounds scary. Is there anything else?

Dietary changes are crucial. You need to eat six small meals a day instead of three big ones. Everything should be low-fat and low-fiber. Fiber is hard to digest and can clump together in a 'paralyzed' stomach, forming a blockage called a bezoar.

Keep them very small and well-chewed, or avoid them during flares. I want to confirm the diagnosis with a Gastric Emptying Study. You'll eat a standardized meal—usually eggs with a small amount of radioactive tracer—and we'll take pictures over four hours to see how much is left in your stomach.
Okay. I just need some relief. The bloating is making me miserable.
Good afternoon, Mrs. Higgins. I see you're back with us. The diarrhea has returned again?

It's the third time this year, Doctor! I finished the antibiotics for my dental implant three weeks ago, and within five days, the watery stools, that horrible smell, and the cramping were all back. I'm so weak I can't even stand up.

I am so sorry. This is a classic case of recurrent Clostridioides difficile, or C. diff. Every time we take general antibiotics, they wipe out the good bacteria in your gut, and the C. diff spores take over. Since this is your third recurrence, we need a more durable solution than just standard Vancomycin.
I've taken the Vancomycin twice now. It works while I'm on it, but the minute I stop, I'm sick again.

That's because the Vancomycin doesn't kill the spores, and your microbiome isn't healthy enough to keep them from sprouting. This time, I'm prescribing Fidaxomicin—it's more targeted and has a lower recurrence rate. I'm also going to start you on a long 'pulse-and-taper' course of Vancomycin to try and catch the spores as they wake up.
What about that stool transplant thing I read about?

Fecal Microbiota Transplant, or FMT, is actually the most effective treatment for multiple recurrences. It has a nearly 90% success rate because it literally replants a healthy colony of bacteria in your colon. Since you're on your third bout, you are officially a candidate.
That sounds... well, a bit gross.

It’s done via colonoscopy while you're asleep, so you don't experience anything. We're also seeing new FDA-approved 'pill' versions of the transplant, like Vowst, which you take by mouth. We can discuss that once we finish this round of Fidaxomicin.
I just want to be able to go to the grocery store without checking where the bathrooms are first.

I hear you. Let's get the stool sample tested today just to confirm it's still C. diff and not something else, but we'll start the Fidaxomicin immediately. Plenty of Pedialyte for the dehydration, please.
Good morning, James. I see you've been having some burning in your stomach. Is it usually before or after you eat?
It's strange, Doctor. If I'm hungry, it's like a fire is lit in my upper belly. But then I'll eat a sandwich or some crackers, and it feels better for maybe an hour or two. Then it comes right back.
Pain that is relieved by eating is often a sign of a duodenal ulcer. Have you noticed your stools looking darker than usual, or perhaps even black?
Now that you mention it, they have been looking very dark, almost like tar. I thought it was just the iron vitamins I started taking.
Black, tarry stools, or melena, is a sign of upper GI bleeding. Are you taking any pain medications like Ibuprofen, Naproxen, or Aspirin for other issues?
I have chronic back pain, so I've been taking about 800 milligrams of Ibuprofen three times a day for the last six months. It's the only way I can get through work.

That's exactly why you're here. Heavy NSAID use is one of the leading causes of peptic ulcers because it strips the protective lining of the stomach. We also need to check for a bacteria called Helicobacter pylori.
Yes. It’s very common and can persist for years, causing inflammation. I'm going to order an EGD to look for the ulcer and take a small biopsy to test specifically for H. pylori.

Then we'll start 'triple therapy'—two different antibiotics and a high-dose PPI for fourteen days. But the most important thing is you must stop the Ibuprofen immediately. Use Tylenol for your back, or we'll discuss other non-NSAID options.
I had no idea the Ibuprofen was doing this. I'll stop today.
Good. I'm also starting you on Pantoprazole 40 milligrams twice a day to help that ulcer heal. I'll see you for the endoscopy on Friday.
Hello, Mrs. Gable. You're holding your left side there. Is that where the pain is most intense?
Yes, Dr. Chen. It started as a dull ache two days ago, but now it's a sharp, stabbing pain right here in my lower left abdomen. I have a chill, and I feel like I'm going to be sick.
I’m going to press down on that area... let me know if it's tender. (Presses)
Ooh! Yes! Very tender. Please don't do that again.
I’m sorry. You have significant tenderness in the left lower quadrant, and your temperature is 100.8. Give your age and history of constipation, this is very suspicious for Diverticulitis.
Diverticulitis? My sister had that. Does that mean my colon is going to burst?

Most cases are 'uncomplicated,' meaning it's just inflammation and maybe a micro-perforation that the body is handling. But we need a CT scan of your abdomen and pelvis to be sure there isn't an abscess or a large perforation.

For an uncomplicated flare like this, we'll start with 'bowel rest'—meaning a clear liquid diet for the next 48 to 72 hours—and a course of oral antibiotics, usually Ciprofloxacin and Metronidazole. If the pain doesn't improve or the fever gets higher, you'll need to go to the ER.
So just liquids? No solid food at all?

Nothing but clear broth, Jell-O, and water until the inflammation settles down. After the pain is completely gone, we'll slowly introduce a high-fiber diet to prevent future flares. But during the flare, fiber is actually your enemy.
I understand. I'll go get the CT scan now.
Good afternoon, Brenda. I understand you've had a few episodes of intense pain lately?

It's the weirdest thing, Doctor. I'll have a normal day, then after dinner—especially if I have something greasy—I get this crushing pain right under my ribs on the right side. It even goes up into my right shoulder blade. It's so bad I can't catch my breath.
Maybe an hour or two, and then it just vanishes like it was never there. But I'm terrified to eat anything now.

That's a classic description of 'biliary colic.' When you eat fat, your gallbladder squeezes to release bile. If you have gallstones, one of those stones can get stuck in the neck of the gallbladder while it's squeezing, causing that intense pressure.
It’s actually quite common in your age group. I'm ordering an ultrasound of your right upper quadrant today. It's the best way to see the stones or any sludge.
And if there are stones, do I need my gallbladder out?

If the stones are causing this kind of pain, the standard treatment is surgery to remove the gallbladder, called a cholecystectomy. Otherwise, the stones can eventually cause an infection or even pancreatitis if they migrate into the common duct.
Can I live without a gallbladder?

Absolutely. Your liver still makes bile; it just drips into the intestine constantly instead of being stored and released in bursts. Most people digest food perfectly fine after surgery. Let's start with the ultrasound first.
Hello, Sophie. We've done a lot of tests—your colonoscopy was normal, your celiac screen was negative, and your labs look great. But you're still having symptoms.

I don't get it, Doctor. If the tests are normal, why do I feel like my stomach is tied in knots? One week I can't go to the bathroom at all, and the next I'm running there six times a day with terrible cramping. The bloating is so bad I can't even zip my jeans by five PM.

What you're describing fits the criteria for Irritable Bowel Syndrome, specifically the 'mixed' type, or IBS-M. It’s what we call a disorder of the 'gut-brain axis.' Your gut is physically healthy, but the way it moves and perceives pain is hypersensitive.
So it's all in my head?
Not at all. The pain is very real. It's just that the wiring is overactive. Have you noticed if your symptoms get worse when you're stressed or anxious?
Definitely. During finals week, it’s a disaster.

That’s very common. I want to try a multidimensional approach. First, a 'Low FODMAP' diet—reducing certain sugars that ferment in your gut and cause gas. Second, I'm prescribing Dicyclomine, which is an anti-spasmodic for those painful cramps.
It should help the pain. For the bloating and the alternating stools, we'll try a specific type of fiber called Psyllium, which actually helps bulk up the loose stools and soften the hard ones.
I'll try anything at this point.
I also recommend some mindfulness or stress-management techniques. Even light yoga can help calm the nervous system in the gut. Let's follow up in six weeks to see how the diet and meds are working.
Good morning, Ryan. I understand you've been having some pain and bleeding during bowel movements?
Doctor, it’s like passing shards of glass. Every time I go, I’m in agony for hours afterward. And there’s always bright red blood on the toilet paper. I’m honestly scared to go to the bathroom.

That 'shards of glass' description is very specific for an anal fissure—a small tear in the lining of the anal canal. The bright red blood is also consistent with that, or potentially internal hemorrhoids. Are you struggling with constipation?
I've always been a bit irregular. I have to strain quite a bit.
The straining is the root cause. It creates the pressure that leads to the hemorrhoids and the tears. I’m going to do a quick external examination and a digital rectal exam to confirm.

(Post-exam) I see a small posterior fissure and some Grade II internal hemorrhoids. The good news is these are very treatable. I'm prescribing a topical Nitroglycerin ointment to help the fissure heal by increasing blood flow and relaxing the muscle.

It will help, but you also need to start Sitz baths—soaking in warm water for ten minutes twice a day. Most importantly, we must stop the straining. I want you on a daily stool softener and a high-fiber supplement.
What if it doesn't get better?

If the fissure is chronic, we can discuss Botox injections and if the hemorrhoids persist, we can do rubber band ligation right here in the office. But 90% of the time, the fiber and sitz baths do the trick. No more reading on the toilet, okay? Five minutes and done.
Ha, okay. Five minutes. I can do that.
Hello, Megan. You've been complaining of extreme bloating and gas, especially right after you eat?

It's so embarrassing, Dr. Chen. I look six months pregnant by two PM every single day. The gas is constant, and it doesn't matter what I eat. Even a salad sets me off. I've also been having some loose stools that are really pale and hard to flush.

That 'immediate' bloating—often within thirty to sixty minutes of eating—is very characteristic of SIBO. Normally, your small intestine is relatively sterile, but in SIBO, bacteria from the colon migrate upward and ferment your food before you can absorb it.
Is that why I'm always tired?

Yes. Those bacteria can compete for nutrients like Vitamin B12 and interfering with fat absorption. I’m ordering a Lactulose Breath Test. You'll drink a sugar solution and blow into a tube every fifteen minutes. We're looking for spikes in hydrogen or methane gas.

We treat it with a very specific antibiotic called Rifaximin. Unlike other antibiotics, it isn't absorbed into your bloodstream; it stays in the small intestine to kill the local overgrowth. We'll also discuss a 'low-fermentation' diet to keep the bacteria from coming back.
I just want my stomach to stay flat for once. Let's do the test.
Good evening, Mr. Henderson. I’m glad you came into the clinic. You had a sudden onset of pain this morning?

It hit me like a ton of bricks around nine AM, Doctor. Really sharp cramping on my left side. I rushed to the bathroom, and instead of a normal movement, I just passed what looked like dark red jelly and some blood clots. It scared the life out of me.

Sudden left-sided pain followed by hematochezia in a patient with a history of atrial fibrillation is highly concerning for Ischemic Colitis. It’s essentially a 'stroke' of the colon where a small blood vessel gets blocked or blood flow drops.

We need to act quickly. I'm referring you to the emergency department for a CT scan with IV contrast to check the blood flow to your bowel wall. Most of the time, it's transient and heals with IV fluids and bowel rest, but we have to ensure there's no full-thickness necrosis.
Please do. I'll call the intake doctor to let them know you're coming.
Hello, Mrs. Patel. I see you're here because of some changes in your bowel habits?

I'm fifty-three, and I'll admit, I've avoided my colonoscopy. But lately, my stools have become very narrow, almost like a pencil. I'm also feeling very weak, and I've lost fifteen pounds since Christmas without trying.

Mrs. Patel, 'pencil-thin' stools and unintentional weight loss are significant alarm symptoms. Your recent labs show your iron levels are dangerously low, which often means there's slow, occult bleeding in the colon. Given you haven't had a screening, we must perform a diagnostic colonoscopy immediately.

It is one of the possibilities we have to rule out. We'll also check a CEA marker in your blood. I'm scheduling your colonoscopy for tomorrow's 'urgent' block. We need to see exactly what's causing that narrowing.
I should have come in years ago.
Let's focus on the next steps. The preparation is the hardest part, but we need a very clean view. My nurse will go over the instructions with you.
Good morning, John. We’ve been monitoring your Hepatitis B for a few years now. Your viral load came back significantly higher this time—over 10,000 international units.
Does that mean the virus is damaging my liver now? I've been feeling a bit more tired lately, and I have a dull ache on my right side.

It suggests the virus has entered an 'active' phase. Even if your liver enzymes are only slightly elevated, a rising viral load increases the long-term risk of cirrhosis and liver cancer. I’m going to order a liver ultrasound today to screen for any nodules.
Will I need to be on medication forever?

Often, yes. Drugs like Tenofovir are very effective at suppressing the virus to 'undetectable' levels, which allows your liver to stay healthy. It's usually one pill a day. We'll start the medication after we get the ultrasound results.
Hello, Clara. We've been looking at your liver enzymes, and they've jumped from 40 to over 250 in just two months. How are you feeling?
I feel like I've been hit by a truck, Doctor. My joints ache, I'm exhausted, and I've noticed these little red spider-like veins on my chest. Am I turning yellow?

Your bilirubin is creeping up, so you have some mild jaundice. The 'spider angiomas' and the joint pain, combined with those high enzymes, point toward Autoimmune Hepatitis. Your immune system is mistakenly attacking your own liver cells.
How do we know for sure?

We checked your ANA and Smooth Muscle Antibodies, which were both positive. But the gold standard is a liver biopsy. I’m going to have the radiologist perform a needle biopsy to look for 'interface hepatitis.'

We treat it by dampening the immune system, usually starting with Prednisone and then transitioning to a drug called Azathioprine. It's very effective, but we have to monitor your labs weekly at first.
I just want the fatigue to go away. Let's do the biopsy.
